thread_tweets.full_text:
.@11x_official's revenue agents are generating hundreds of millions of dollars in pipeline for their customers.

Here's their full stack of internal agents, AI they run inside their own company, so that they can spend maximum time building agents for customers.

We sat down with CEO @prabhavjain and went through their full internal AI operating stack:

thread_tweets.text:
1/ Customer qualification skill

When @prabhavjain took over from CTO to CEO, he made a rule:
They are only selling to customers who will see incredible value from 11x.
So he built a Claude skill every sales rep uses.

It pulls in emails, call notes, Slack messages exchanged with prospects.
It considers industry-specific nuances and patterns from past successful customers.

If it's missing information, it tells the rep what questions to ask.
The agent gives the rep a confidence score whether the customer is a fit or not. Reps only close deals with confidence >85%.
thread_tweets.full_text:
1/ Customer qualification skill

When @prabhavjain took over from CTO to CEO, he made a rule:
They are only selling to customers who will see incredible value from 11x.
So he built a Claude skill every sales rep uses.

It pulls in emails, call notes, Slack messages exchanged with prospects.
It considers industry-specific nuances and patterns from past successful customers.

If it's missing information, it tells the rep what questions to ask.
The agent gives the rep a confidence score whether the customer is a fit or not. Reps only close deals with confidence >85%.

thread_tweets.text:
2/ Deal handoff agent

Customer satisfaction = expectations (what was promised during sales) minus reality (what the account team helps deliver)

To make sure every detail of what was promised upfront is communicated to the post-sale team, they built an agent.

It runs on custom code and you can assign it to grade a deal in Slack by @ mentioning it.

Claude integrations pull every call from @meetgranola, Slack, and email exchanged across the whole cycle.

Then it generates a Notion doc and a kickoff deck.

Agents are good at context, humans are good at service.
thread_tweets.full_text:

Agents are good at context, humans are good at service.

thread_tweets.text:
3/ "Lemmings"

11x runs a company-wide fleet of internal codebase-read agents they call lemmings. The non-engineering team dispatches lemmings via Slack to answer questions and build a handful of customer-facing docs:

• Create customer docs based on codebase, tailored to specific customer
• Generate insights for a customer, and put into a polished doc
• Debug and optimize a customer's setup
• Answer customer questions based on product and outcome data
• Create decks for quarterly customer reviews

This is built with @LangChain's LangSmith Fleet, which lets you describe in plain english a fleet of agents that work across your internal code and tools, and at mention them from Slack.

thread_tweets.text:
4/ Coding agent + test/review harness

Like all startups, 11x uses coding agents heavily.

But 11x also realized that as coding agents wrote more PRs, the bottleneck would move to verifying PRs.

So early on, they decided to invest heavily in building their own code harness.

The code harness verifies PRs created by agents by:
• Loading up a full virtual environment w/ @coderhq + @Docker
• Figures out what the PR is about and makes a test plan
• Runs e2e automated tests in the virtual environment via @playwrightweb
• Runs existing suite of regression tests
• Puts evidence of the new feature working via screenshot in PR
• Has mock data that mimics production (including volume) and ensures performance efficiency doesn’t drop with any new PR

thread_tweets.text:
5/ The agentic office of the CEO

CEO @prabhavjain built a simple fleet of personal agents with intel for each day, all running on Claude Cowork scheduled jobs.

The first is a customer brief, delivered via Slack DM each week. It ingests every customer call via the @meetgranola MCP. This gives him a weekly update on what matters for customers right now.

The second is a meeting prep agent that reads email, Slack, Granola, conducts research and preps him for the next day's meetings.

The third combs through his Slack every evening, and flags unresolved threads. Resolution isn't always explicit, it could be just an emoji reaction, or it could be in a different DM than the main discussion thread, so the agent has to reason.

A fourth is a weekly market pulse. It pulls from the internet, and also from objections and competitors mentioned in that week's deals in calls and emails. This gives him a pulse on what the field is seeing.

thread_tweets.text:
One thing to really like about this stack is it's simple; it's not over-engineered.

Outside of the code harness, it's mostly Claude Cowork + MCPs running at timed intervals. They are getting value by having good ideas of what to automate, not by having fancier tools.

The framework: basic, boring tech that just works, doesn't break, and you don't have to fuss with.

We're excited to lead Netris' Series A.

Data center networking may be changing even more quickly than compute. It is enormously complex to manage, and when it fails, a data center full of expensive GPUs stops working.

@netris_io built a powerful and robust network management solution for GPU clouds or anyone running a large GPU data center.

What impressed us most was the scale: operators run close-to-$1B data centers on Netris software - and the company has spent 8 years earning that trust.

We’re excited to partner with @alex_saroyan, Arsen Arakelyan, Tigran Martirosyan, and the Netris team to take networking into the age of AI.

By @appenz, @RaghuRaghuram, and @JasonSCui

Today, we're announcing our $15 million Series A, led by @a16z.

In 2018, @tmartiro , @ars_arakelyan , and I started @netris_io . The hyperscalers had proven what's possible when you automate the network at scale, but it took custom tooling and armies of engineers. We wanted to https://t.co/sXsxPxlXIs
tweets.full_text:
We're excited to lead Netris' Series A.

Data center networking may be changing even more quickly than compute. It is enormously complex to manage, and when it fails, a data center full of expensive GPUs stops working.

@netris_io built a powerful and robust network management solution for GPU clouds or anyone running a large GPU data center.

What impressed us most was the scale: operators run close-to-$1B data centers on Netris software - and the company has spent 8 years earning that trust.

We’re excited to partner with @alex_saroyan, Arsen Arakelyan, Tigran Martirosyan, and the Netris team to take networking into the age of AI.

By @appenz, @RaghuRaghuram, and @JasonSCui

tweets.text:
BREAKING: Iran is pitching a plan where it would charge fees along with Persian Gulf countries for vessels looking to transit the Strait of Hormuz, per WSJ.

Details include:

1. Iran estimates it could make $40 billion per year by charging for security, safety, and environmental services in Hormuz

2. Iran is pitching the idea to the wider Middle East and as far afield as Beijing

3. "Everyone needs to know that management of the Strait will never return to the way it was before," Iran's Speaker of the Parliament said

4. Iran has already set up an insurance firm it says shippers must use to cross the Strait of Hormuz

Iran appears to be preparing for fees to transit Hormuz after the US-Iran 60-day MoU expires.

🇫🇷 🇲🇨 Très heureux de partager ce portrait publié dans @LePoint (via @philippinerbt), qui revient sur mon parcours, ma méthode et ma vision de la macroéconomie.

Derrière les classements, les prévisions et les chiffres, il y a surtout une conviction profonde que la macroéconomie n’est pas un exercice de certitude mais plutôt un travail permanent de discipline, de probabilités et d’humilité.

Prévoir n’est pas chercher �� avoir raison contre le marché mais plutôt essayer de comprendre plus vite ce qui change, d’identifier les signaux faibles, de distinguer le bruit de l’information utile, et d’accepter que chaque scénario doit être révisé dès que les données l’exigent.

Dans un monde où les cycles sont plus courts, les chocs plus fréquents et les réactions de marché parfois plus violentes, l’analyse macro doit rester à la fois rigoureuse, réactive et profondément connectée au réel à travers l'emploi, l'inflation, la politique monétaire, la consommation, le crédit, les flux et la géopolitique.

C’est cette approche que j’essaie de défendre au quotidien, celle d'une macroéconomie utile, opérationnelle, fondée sur les données, mais jamais déconnectée du terrain.

Merci au Point et surtout à Philippine Robert pour ce portrait, et à toutes celles et ceux qui suivent, challengent et enrichissent chaque jour mes analyses.

➡ Lien en commentaire:

Dans un monde où les cycles sont plus courts, les chocs plus fréquents et les réactions de marché parfois plus violentes, l’analyse macro doit rester à la fois rigoureuse, réactive et profondément connectée au réel à travers l'emploi, l'inflation, la politique monétaire, la consommation, le crédit, les flux et la géopolitique.

C’est cette approche que j’essaie de défendre au quotidien, celle d'une macroéconomie utile, opérationnelle, fondée sur les données, mais jamais déconnectée du terrain.

Merci au Point et surtout à Philippine Robert pour ce portrait, et à toutes celles et ceux qui suivent, challengent et enrichissent chaque jour mes analyses.
